attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old woman presents to the maternity assessment unit with concerns that she might be in labour. She is currently 33 weeks and 6 days pregnant. She reports intermittent lower back pain since this morning that is becoming more frequent, as well as intermittent vaginal spotting. This is her first pregnancy, and it has been uncomplicated prior to today. She does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears well. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Her uterus is soft and non-tender. A sterile speculum examination is performed. The cervical os is closed with no pooling of fluid and no blood visible. A fetal fibronectin (fFN) test is negative. Cardiotocography (CTG) shows a normal fetal heart rate pattern with no signs of uterine contractions.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
options: Start nifedipine (20 mg orally) immediately until 37 weeks
Start intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.8 g every 4 hours to prevent chorioamnionitis
Prepare for immediate delivery in a tertiary centre
attributes.explanation:
This question focuses on the diagnosis and management of suspected preterm labour and the utilisation of fetal fibronectin (fFN). Symptoms of preterm labour can be vague, including back pain, pelvic pressure, vaginal spotting, or regular uterine activity. A speculum examination is almost always required and allows visualisation of the cervix, inspection for bleeding, and the ability to perform both high vaginal swabs for microscopy, culture, and sensitivity (MC&S) and a fFN test.

fFN is a protein found between the fetal chorion and maternal decidua which is usually absent from 24-36 weeks until established labour when it becomes positive. A negative test has a 97% negative predictive value for preterm delivery in the next 7 days. Thus, this patient's negative result means it is very unlikely she is in preterm labour. The normal CTG and soft, non-tender uterus are also reassuring signs. Continuing as normal is the most appropriate plan of management as preterm labour is unlikely.

Starting nifedipine is only indicated in the presence of regular and strong contractions as a tocolytic. In this case, there is no evidence of regular contractions on CTG or clinical examination.

Intravenous benzylpenicillin is unnecessary as the patient has no signs of ruptured membranes and has been feeling well, making chorioamnionitis unlikely.

Intramuscular betamethasone (corticosteroids) is indicated in pregnancies <35 weeks where there is a risk of preterm birth within 7 days, such as preterm premature rupture of membranes (PPROM) or signs of preterm labour with a positive fFN. This patient does not fit any of these categories and does not need steroids.

Preparing for immediate delivery in a tertiary centre is not indicated as there is no history to suggest fetal distress, and the CTG is normal. Ideally, delivery should occur after 37 weeks unless there are specific indications for earlier delivery.

Should her back pain persist or her condition change, then further investigation and management might need to be considered. However, based on the current clinical picture, reassurance and continuation of normal antenatal care is appropriate.
learningPoints: A negative fetal fibronectin is an accurate predictor of the unlikelihood of a patient going into labour in the 7 days following the test.
Normal CTG and a soft, non-tender uterus are reassuring signs in the assessment of suspected preterm labour.
references: Mahomed, K, Anwar, S, Geer, JE, Ballard, E & Okano, S 2019, ‘Evaluation of fetal fibronectin for threatened preterm labour in reducing inappropriate interventions’, Australian and New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology, vol. 59, pp. 523-7.
South Australian Perinatal Practice Guideline 2020, Preterm Labour, Department of Health, Adelaide, viewed 18/8/2020.
